citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The enrofloxacin the facility has been using for treating abscesses expired Feb 2025. Medicines used to prevent or treat
disease must be properly stored, labeled, and not expired in order for them to appropriately and effectively prevent,
control, and/or treat diseases.
Correct by: 22 April 2026

citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
There was an approximately 1-inch diameter bloody wound on the right back lateral flank of an adult rabbit identified by
the inspector. There were several small scabs and a small piece of white chunky material on surface of wound. The
facility reported they had not observed the wound nor started treatment. The rabbit appeared to eating and moving around
the enclosure normally.
Failure to promptly identify injuries delays treatment and may cause undue pain and distress in animals with such
conditions. Additionally, ensure that daily observation of animals is adequate to identify any signs of poor health.
Correct by: 22 April 2026

Many sections of the interior wall of the Rabbitry have exposed and damaged wood at the bottom that cannot be
adequately cleaned and sanitized. The paint has worn away, the wood is broken and the surfaces are irregular and damp
with a green film present. There are also several approximately 2 to 4 inch diameter holes in the bottom of the walls.
Surfaces that cannot be cleaned and disinfected contribute to an unhealthy environment for the animals because they can
harbor harmful microbes, attract pests and vermin, and release fumes that can be irritating or harmful to the animals. The
licensee must ensure that interior surfaces of indoor housing facilities are constructed and maintained so that they are
substantially impervious to moisture and may be readily sanitized.
Correct by: 13 May 2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with Facility Representative.
n

3.51(d) 
   
Facilities, indoor. 
Many sections of the interior wall of the Rabbitry have exposed and damaged wood at the bottom that cannot be 
adequately cleaned and sanitized. The paint has worn away, the wood is broken and the surfaces are irregular and damp 
with a green film present. There are also several approximately 2 to 4 inch diameter holes in the bottom of the walls. 
Surfaces that cannot be cleaned and disinfected contribute to an unhealthy environment for the animals because they can 
harbor harmful microbes, attract pests and vermin, and release fumes that can be irritating or harmful to the animals. The 
licensee must ensure that interior surfaces of indoor housing facilities are constructed and maintained so that they are 
substantially impervious to moisture and may be readily sanitized. 
Correct by: 13 May 2026

This inspection and exit interview were conducted with Facility Representative.
